content.text: Quantization-aware training (QAT) simulates the numerical errors introduced by low-precision quantization during the training forward pass, allowing the model's weights to adapt to those errors before the final conversion to integer format. Unlike post-training quantization (PTQ), which quantizes a frozen model after training, QAT incorporates quantization noise into the gradient signal and produces weights that are inherently more tolerant of the precision reduction. The result is typically 0.5–2% better accuracy than PTQ at INT4, at the cost of a full or partial training run.

QAT introduces fake quantization nodes into the computation graph. During the forward pass, weights and activations are quantized to the target precision (e.g. INT8) and then immediately dequantized back to floating-point, so that the compute graph still operates in FP32/BF16 but experiences the same information loss as the target integer format:

The fake-quantized weight Q(W) is in FP32 but carries only 8-bit information content. The difference W - Q(W) is the quantization error that the model must learn to compensate for. After QAT, when the model is actually deployed in INT8, it has already adapted to this error distribution.

attrs.alt: QAT training loop: FP32 latent weights pass through a fake-quantize node before the linear layer. The Straight-Through Estimator (STE) makes round() differentiable by treating its gradient as 1 during backprop.

content.text: The round() function in fake quantization has zero gradient almost everywhere — it is a piecewise constant staircase. This means gradients cannot flow backward through the fake-quantize node without a workaround. The Straight-Through Estimator (STE) resolves this by defining a surrogate gradient:

In practice, STE simply passes the upstream gradient through the quantize node unchanged, as if the node were an identity function — with the gradient set to zero only when the weight falls outside the representable range (clamp region):
QAT for LLMs follows a three-phase workflow:

QAT begins from a model that has already converged at full precision. Starting from random initialization with QAT is theoretically possible but extremely slow — the model must simultaneously learn to represent data and adapt to quantization noise.

Replace linear layers (or all weight tensors) with QAT-wrapped versions that apply fake quantization during the forward pass. Activations can be fake-quantized too, but weight quantization alone captures the majority of the benefit.
Fine-tune for a fraction of the original training budget:

Run 1–10% of the original training steps with a low learning rate (1e-5 to 5e-5). The model adjusts its weight distribution to cluster values near the finite set of representable integers. A cosine learning rate schedule with a short warmup (1% of steps) works well.
Remove fake-quantize nodes, compute final per-tensor or per-channel scales from the adapted weights, and convert to INT8/INT4 storage. The real quantization replaces the fake nodes at this point.

# QAT with PyTorch's built-in quantization framework (torch.ao)
import torch
from torch.ao.quantization import get_default_qat_qconfig, prepare_qat, convert

model = ...  # pretrained FP32 model

# Step 1: Attach QAT configuration
model.train()
model.qconfig = get_default_qat_qconfig("fbgemm")  # fbgemm for x86; qnnpack for ARM
model_prepared = prepare_qat(model)  # inserts fake-quantize and observer nodes

# Step 2: Fine-tune with fake quantization active
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model_prepared.parameters(), lr=2e-5, weight_decay=0.01)
scheduler = torch.optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=num_steps)

for step, batch in enumerate(train_loader):
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    outputs = model_prepared(**batch)
    loss = outputs.loss
    loss.backward()
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model_prepared.parameters(), 1.0)
    optimizer.step()
    scheduler.step()

# Step 3: Convert to real INT8
model_prepared.eval()
model_int8 = convert(model_prepared)
# model_int8 now stores true INT8 weights and uses integer arithmetic during inference
QAT for LLMs with Transformers / PEFT

Training multi-billion parameter LLMs with QAT requires significant GPU memory — each weight exists in both FP32 (latent) and a fake-quantized FP32 copy during forward pass, roughly doubling the activation memory. In practice, QAT for LLMs is almost always combined with LoRA (parameter-efficient fine-tuning) to limit the number of trainable parameters:

# QAT + LoRA for a 7B LLM using bitsandbytes + PEFT
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training
import torch

# Load with 4-bit NF4 quantization (QLoRA baseline — close to QAT in effect)
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",          # NormalFloat4 — better than linear INT4 for normally-distributed weights
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,     # nested quantization of scale factors
)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3-8B",
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)

# Prepare for k-bit training (casts layer norms to FP32, enables gradient checkpointing)
model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model, use_gradient_checkpointing=True)

# Add LoRA adapters on top of quantized base
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj", "k_proj", "o_proj", "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# Trainable params: ~20M (0.25% of 8B) — only LoRA adapters; quantized base is frozen

QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) demonstrated that NF4 (NormalFloat4) — a quantization format derived from the quantiles of the normal distribution rather than linear spacing — better represents the Gaussian-distributed weights of pretrained LLMs. NF4 with double quantization achieves near-FP16 quality for fine-tuning adapter layers while reducing the 7B model from 14 GB to ~5 GB VRAM.

Standard INT4 quantization maps the range [W_min, W_max] to 16 equally-spaced integers. This is efficient for uniform distributions but wasteful for the Gaussian-distributed weights of LLMs — most weight values cluster near zero, while the extreme quantization levels (representing rarely-occurring large weights) waste representational capacity. NF4 remedies this by placing quantization levels at the quantiles of the standard normal distribution:

where Phi is the standard normal CDF. This places more quantization levels near zero (where LLM weights are densest) and fewer at the extremes, minimising quantization error for typical weight distributions. The table below shows the difference in level placement for 4-bit quantization:
Gaussian distributions — wastes levels at tails
When QAT Is Worth the Cost
Given that QAT requires a training run while PTQ requires only calibration, the decision depends on accuracy requirements and available compute:
PTQ achieves near-FP16 at INT8; QAT overhead not justified
1–3% PPL degradation acceptable for most deployments
INT4 inference, high-stakes tasks (medical, legal, coding)
Recover the 1–2% accuracy gap; worth the extra training
INT4 inference for a fine-tuned model
Fine-tune and quantize simultaneously; no separate step
PTQ quality at 2–3 bits is generally unusable; QAT essential
Fixed hardware constraints; squeezed accuracy ceiling matters

import math, torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from datasets import load_dataset

def compare_models(fp16_path: str, qat_path: str, n_samples: int = 100):
    """Compare FP16 baseline vs QAT model on perplexity and a downstream task."""
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(fp16_path)
    data = load_dataset("wikitext", "wikitext-2-raw-v1", split="test")
    text = "\n\n".join(data["text"][:n_samples])
    enc = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt", max_length=4096, truncation=True)

    results = {}
    for label, path in [("fp16", fp16_path), ("qat", qat_path)]:
        model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(path, torch_dtype=torch.float16).cuda().eval()
        with torch.no_grad():
            out = model(enc.input_ids.cuda(), labels=enc.input_ids.cuda())
        ppl = math.exp(out.loss.item())
        results[label] = ppl
        print(f"{label:6s}  PPL = {ppl:.3f}")

    delta = (results["qat"] - results["fp16"]) / results["fp16"] * 100
    print(f"PPL degradation: {delta:+.1f}%")
    # Acceptable: < 3% for general use, < 1% for high-stakes applications
    return results

# Example results (Llama-3 8B on WikiText-2):
# fp16   PPL = 6.14
# qat    PPL = 6.30  (+2.6%)   <- QAT INT4
# ptq    PPL = 6.48  (+5.5%)   <- GPTQ INT4 (for comparison)
Using full LR during QAT fine-tuning
Use LR ≤ 1e-5; QAT is a fine-tune, not a fresh train
Skipping the STE; using true gradients
Zero gradients from round(); training stalls
Always use STE (torch.ao handles this automatically)
Exclude embedding and LM head from QAT nodes
Running QAT from scratch instead of from pretrained
Always start QAT from a pretrained FP32 checkpoint
Converting to INT before QAT is complete
Model hasn't adapted; no benefit over PTQ
Complete all QAT fine-tuning epochs before convert()
Not freezing batch-norm / layer-norm running stats
Scale drift during QAT destabilises training
Set model.eval() for norms during QAT, or use STE for norms too
Native PyTorch QAT with FX graph mode
Custom architectures, research, export to TorchScript / ONNX
LLM fine-tuning with QAT effect at fraction of cost
CPU deployment, Intel Xeon / Gaudi targets
Server inference on NVIDIA GPUs with INT8 Tensor Cores

Weight quantization alone (W4A16 or W8A16) leaves activation tensors in floating-point during inference. For maximum throughput on hardware with INT8 Tensor Cores (NVIDIA A100, H100, Ada Lovelace), activations must also be quantized (W8A8 or W4A8). Activation quantization is harder because:
Activation magnitudes vary per-input, unlike weights which are fixed after training. A static scale computed during calibration may be wrong at inference time.
LLMs above 6.7B parameters develop systematic outliers — specific hidden dimensions that are 10–100× larger than the median, causing catastrophic per-tensor INT8 clipping.
The activation distribution at position 0 (BOS token) differs from position 512, making a single per-tensor scale inaccurate across sequence positions.

QAT handles these issues more gracefully than PTQ because the model can learn to suppress outlier channels during training. When fake-quantize nodes are inserted on activations, the backward pass penalises the model for producing values outside the representable range — pushing the weight update to reduce outlier magnitude:

import torch
from torch.ao.quantization import QConfig, MovingAverageMinMaxObserver, HistogramObserver

# QAT config with per-channel weight quantization and dynamic activation scales
qconfig = QConfig(
    activation=MovingAverageMinMaxObserver.with_args(
        dtype=torch.qint8,
        qscheme=torch.per_tensor_affine,   # asymmetric — handles non-zero-centered activations
        averaging_constant=0.1,            # EMA of observed min/max
    ),
    weight=torch.ao.quantization.default_per_channel_weight_observer,
)

# Apply to model (FX-based graph mode — handles modules without explicit wrappers)
import torch.ao.quantization.quantize_fx as quantize_fx
from torch.ao.quantization import QConfigMapping

qconfig_mapping = QConfigMapping().set_global(qconfig)
example_inputs = torch.randint(0, 32000, (1, 128))  # dummy input for tracing

model_fused = quantize_fx.fuse_fx(model)             # fuse BN+ReLU, LayerNorm, etc.
model_prepared = quantize_fx.prepare_qat_fx(
    model_fused, qconfig_mapping, example_inputs
)

# Fine-tune model_prepared on your dataset, then:
model_quantized = quantize_fx.convert_fx(model_prepared)

The standard STE approach treats the quantization scale s as a hyperparameter fixed by the min-max of each weight tensor. Learned Step Size Quantization (LSQ, Esser et al., 2020) instead makes the scale itself a trainable parameter, updated jointly with the weights during QAT:

where the gradient through the scale is computed by differentiating the dequantized weight with respect to s using STE for the round() component. LSQ converges to tighter quantization ranges than fixed-scale QAT and typically recovers an additional 0.3–0.7% accuracy at INT4 compared to fixed-scale STE:

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LSQFakeQuantize(nn.Module):
    """Learned Step-Size Quantization — scale s is a trainable parameter."""
    def __init__(self, bits: int = 8, symmetric: bool = True):
        super().__init__()
        self.bits = bits
        self.symmetric = symmetric
        max_int = 2 ** (bits - 1) - 1
        self.Qn = -max_int - 1
        self.Qp = max_int
        # Initialise scale as learnable parameter
        self.scale = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(1))

    def forward(self, W: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        s = self.scale.abs() + 1e-8   # ensure positive

        # Scale gradient by 1 / sqrt(num_elements * Qp) — LSQ gradient normalisation
        grad_scale = 1.0 / (W.numel() * self.Qp) ** 0.5
        s_scaled = s * grad_scale + (s - s * grad_scale).detach()  # straight-through for scaling

        W_scaled = W / s_scaled
        W_clipped = W_scaled.clamp(self.Qn, self.Qp)
        W_rounded = W_clipped.round() + (W_clipped - W_clipped.round()).detach()  # STE for round
        return W_rounded * s_scaled

Google's Gemma 2 2B (2024) is distilled from Gemma 2 27B and trained with aggressive quantization-awareness as part of the fine-tuning pipeline. Its deployment targets include on-device inference on Android phones (Pixel 8 Pro, which has 12 GB unified memory). The key QAT decisions reported in the technical report:
Start from the Gemma 2 27B-distilled 2B checkpoint (already incorporating knowledge distillation soft labels).
Apply INT8 QAT for embedding, attention, and feed-forward layers using per-channel weight quantization and dynamic activation quantization.
Use a cosine schedule with 5% warmup and learning rate of 1e-5 for 10K steps on a curated 50B-token dataset.
Evaluate on MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval, and a device-specific benchmark after each QAT checkpoint.
Export via TensorFlow Lite with INT8 kernels; the 2B model occupies 1.3 GB on-device vs 4 GB in BF16.

The resulting model achieves 52.4% on MMLU (vs 51.3% for BF16 GPTQ PTQ) and runs at 30 tokens/sec on a Pixel 8 Pro — demonstrating that QAT is essential for recovering accuracy in the aggressive compression required for on-device deployment.
Much lower than pretraining; start at 2e-5 and reduce on plateau
More steps = better adaptation but diminishing returns past 5%
Short warmup to avoid destabilising fake-quant nodes at start
Maintain gradient estimation quality; use gradient accumulation
Same as fine-tuning; prevents overfit to calibration-like distribution
Delay fake-quant during warmup to stabilise initial loss
Smaller group = better accuracy, higher memory for scales
Critical — STE gradients can be large when many weights are at clamp boundary

Quantization-aware training embeds simulated quantization error into the training forward pass via fake-quantize nodes, and uses the Straight-Through Estimator to propagate gradients through the non-differentiable round() operation. The model's weights adapt to the quantization grid during fine-tuning, producing an INT4 or INT8 model with 0.5–2% higher accuracy than post-training quantization methods at the same bit width. The main practical form for LLMs is QLoRA — NF4 base quantization with LoRA adapters — which achieves QAT-level quality at a fraction of the memory cost. Use QAT when PTQ quality is insufficient, when deploying at sub-4-bit precision, or when the target hardware has tight accuracy requirements that the 1–3% PTQ gap cannot satisfy.
